blocks.text: So all who were at court used to do obeisance to Haman, for so the king had commanded to be done. Mordecai, however, did not do obeisance.
2 : The reasons for Mordecai's refusal to are unclear but possibly explained in the Gk by Mordecai's prayer (13.14) or by the fact that Jews and Agagites were traditionally enemies; see 12.6n.
Then the king's courtiers said to Mordecai, “Mordecai, why do you disobey the king's command?”
Day after day they spoke to him, but he would not listen to them. Then they informed Haman that Mordecai was resisting the king's command. Mordecai had told them that he was a Jew.
4 : , wording reminiscent of Joseph's resistance to the blandishments of Potiphar's wife (Gen 39.10).
So when Haman learned that Mordecai was not doing obeisance to him, he became furiously angry,
and plotted to destroy all the Jews under Artaxerxes’ rule.
6 : day, a correction of the Gk's “thirteenth” (see the Hebrew text 3.13; 8.12; 9.1; cf. Gk 16.20; 8.12). , February-March.

In the twelfth year of King Artaxerxes Haman came to a decision by casting lots, taking the days and the months one by one, to fix on one day to destroy the whole race of Mordecai. The lot fell on the fourteenth day of the month of Adar.

7 : , Esther has been queen for five years. The Heb word for , “pur” (pl. “purim”), is borrowed from Akkadian. Haman resorts to this common form of divination ( 3.128) known also to the Israelites (1 Sam 14.42). , February-March. Instead of the , 8.12 of the Gk and Heb rightly cite the thirteenth.

Then Hamanc said to King Artaxerxes, “There is a certain nation scattered among the other nations in all your kingdom; their laws are different from those of every other nation, and they do not keep the laws of the king. It is not expedient for the king to tolerate them.

8 : Haman's manipulation of the pliable king (see 1.16–20) begins with the truth: the Jews were indeed (Zech 7.14), and like other subject peoples in the Persian empire, their were different (Deut 4.5–6). He slides into falsehood in asserting that Jews .
If it pleases the king, let it be decreed that they are to be destroyed, and I will pay ten thousand talents of silver into the king's treasury.”
9 : , equal in value to more than a hundred million US dollars.
So the king took off his signet ring and gave it to Haman to seal the decree that was to be written against the Jews.
10 : The king's gave Haman unlimited power to sign documents in the king's name (8.2,8; cf. Gen 41.41–42; 1 Kings 21.8–9).
The king told Haman, “Keep the money, and do whatever you want with that nation.”

So on the thirteenth day of the first month the king's secretaries were summoned, and in accordance with Haman's instructions they wrote in the name of King Artaxerxes to the magistrates and the governors in every province from India to Ethiopia. There were one hundred twenty-seven provinces in all, and the governors were addressed each in his own language.
Instructions were sent by couriers throughout all the empire of Artaxerxes to destroy the Jewish people on a given day of the twelfth month, which is Adar, and to plunder their goods. ADDITION B
3.1–13: Haman plots to annihilate the Jews.
13 : , the Persian empire's communication system was renowned ( 8.98). , the thirteenth (see 8.12, Gk and Heb). , February-March.
The matter was expedited also in Susa. And while the king and Haman caroused together, the city of Susa was thrown into confusion.
3.14–15: Haman and the king celebrate the publication of the decree.
